text:
Since I use standard scaled data, interpretability of the thresholds is unclear. Can you somehow fix it? # Create and train decision tree classifier
tree_model = DecisionTreeClassifier(random_state=42)
tree_model.fit(X_train, y_train)

# Make predictions
y_pred = tree_model.predict(X_test)

# Evaluate the model
accuracy = accuracy_score(y_test, y_pred)
print(f"Decision Tree Accuracy: {accuracy:.4f}")
print("\nClassification Report:")
print(classification_report(y_test, y_pred))

# Create confusion matrix
cm = confusion_matrix(y_test, y_pred)
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 6))
sns.heatmap(
    cm,
    annot=True,
    fmt="d",
    cmap="Blues",
    xticklabels=["No Disease", "Disease"],
    yticklabels=["No Disease", "Disease"],
)
plt.xlabel("Predicted")
plt.ylabel("Actual")
plt.title("Confusion Matrix")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

# Visualize the decision tree
feature_names = df.drop("HeartDisease", axis=1).columns
plt.figure(figsize=(20, 10))
plot_tree(
    tree_model,
    max_depth=2,
    feature_names=feature_names,
    class_names=["No Disease", "Disease"],
    filled=True,
    rounded=True,
)
plt.title("Decision Tree Visualization")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
# Fixing Interpretability Issue with Standardized Data in Decision Trees

When using standardized data with a decision tree, the threshold values in the visualization won't be easily interpretable since they're in terms of standard deviations rather than the original units. Here are two approaches to fix this issue:

## Option 1: Train the decision tree on non-standardized data

Since decision trees don't require standardization (unlike many other ML algorithms), you can simply train them on the original data:

```python
# Get original feature columns (not standardized)
X_orig = df.drop("HeartDisease", axis=1)
y = df["HeartDisease"]

# Split the data
X_train_orig, X_test_orig, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X_orig, y, test_size=0.2, random_state=42)

# Train decision tree on original data
tree_model = DecisionTreeClassifier(random_state=42)
tree_model.fit(X_train_orig, y_train)

# Make predictions and evaluate using original data
y_pred = tree_model.predict(X_test_orig)
print(f"Decision Tree Accuracy: {accuracy_score(y_test, y_pred):.4f}")
print("\nClassification Report:")
print(classification_report(y_test, y_pred))

# Visualize the decision tree with original feature values
plt.figure(figsize=(20, 10))
plot_tree(
    tree_model,
    max_depth=2,
    feature_names=feature_names,
    class_names=["No Disease", "Disease"],
    filled=True,
    rounded=True,
)
plt.title("Decision Tree with Original Feature Values")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

## Option 2: Transform thresholds back to original scale

If you need to keep using the standardized model, you can transform the thresholds in the visualization back to the original scale:

```python
# Get the scaler means and standard deviations
# Assuming you've used a StandardScaler stored in a variable called 'scaler'
means = scaler.mean_
stds = scaler.scale_

# Function to convert standardized threshold to original scale
def convert_threshold(feature_idx, std_threshold):
    return (std_threshold * stds[feature_idx]) + means[feature_idx]

# Create a custom tree visualization function
def plot_tree_with_original_thresholds(tree, feature_names, class_names, max_depth=2):
    n_nodes = tree.tree_.node_count
    children_left = tree.tree_.children_left
    children_right = tree.tree_.children_right
    feature = tree.tree_.feature
    threshold = tree.tree_.threshold
    
    # Draw the tree structure
    def draw_tree(node_id, depth, x, y, width):
        if depth > max_depth:
            return
            
        if children_left[node_id] != children_right[node_id]:  # Not a leaf node
            feat_idx = feature[node_id]
            feat_name = feature_names[feat_idx]
            orig_threshold = convert_threshold(feat_idx, threshold[node_id])
            
            # Display decision with original threshold
            plt.text(x, y, f"{feat_name} <= {orig_threshold:.2f}", fontsize=10, 
                      bbox=dict(facecolor='lightblue', alpha=0.5))
            
            # Draw left branch
            plt.plot([x, x-width/2], [y-0.1, y-0.5], 'k-')
            draw_tree(children_left[node_id], depth+1, x-width/2, y-1, width/2)
            
            # Draw right branch
            plt.plot([x, x+width/2], [y-0.1, y-0.5], 'k-')
            draw_tree(children_right[node_id], depth+1, x+width/2, y-1, width/2)
        else:  # Leaf node
            class_prob = tree.tree_.value[node_id][0]
            class_idx = np.argmax(class_prob)
            class_name = class_names[class_idx]
            plt.text(x, y, f"Class: {class_name}", fontsize=10,
                      bbox=dict(facecolor='lightgreen', alpha=0.5))
    
    plt.figure(figsize=(15, 10))
    plt.axis('off')
    draw_tree(0, 0, 0.5, 0.9, 0.4)
    plt.title("Decision Tree with Original Feature Thresholds")
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()

# Use the custom function to visualize the tree
plot_tree_with_original_thresholds(
    tree_model,
    feature_names,
    class_names=["No Disease", "Disease"],
    max_depth=2
)
```

## Recommendation

I recommend using Option 1 since it's simpler and decision trees don't need standardization. This approach will directly give you interpretable thresholds in the original feature scale.

If you still need to use standardized data for compatibility with other parts of your pipeline, implement Option 2 to convert the thresholds back to the original scale for visualization purposes.
Option 2 but do not plot, just get me some nice print
# Printing Decision Tree Rules with Original (Unstandardized) Thresholds

Here's a function that will print the decision tree rules in an interpretable format by converting the standardized thresholds back to their original scale:

```python
def print_decision_tree_rules(tree_model, feature_names, class_names, scaler, max_depth=3):
    """
    Print decision tree rules with thresholds in the original scale
    
    Parameters:
    -----------
    tree_model : DecisionTreeClassifier
        The trained decision tree model
    feature_names : list
        List of feature names
    class_names : list
        List of class names
    scaler : StandardScaler
        The scaler used to standardize the data
    max_depth : int
        Maximum depth to print
    """
    # Get the scaler means and standard deviations
    means = scaler.mean_
    stds = scaler.scale_
    
    # Get tree structure
    tree_ = tree_model.tree_
    
    # Convert standardized threshold to original scale
    def convert_threshold(feature_idx, std_threshold):
        return (std_threshold * stds[feature_idx]) + means[feature_idx]
    
    # Function to recursively extract and print rules
    def extract_rules(node_id, depth, parent_rules=None):
        if parent_rules is None:
            parent_rules = []
        
        # If we've reached max depth or a leaf node, print the rule
        if depth >= max_depth or tree_.children_left[node_id] == tree_.children_right[node_id]:
            # Leaf node - get the class prediction
            value = tree_.value[node_id][0]
            class_idx = np.argmax(value)
            class_prob = value[class_idx] / np.sum(value)
            
            if parent_rules:
                rule_str = " AND ".join(parent_rules)
                print(f"IF {rule_str} THEN class = {class_names[class_idx]} (probability: {class_prob:.2f})")
            else:
                print(f"Root node predicts class = {class_names[class_idx]} (probability: {class_prob:.2f})")
            
            return
        
        # Not a leaf node, so get the decision feature and threshold
        feature_idx = tree_.feature[node_id]
        feature_name = feature_names[feature_idx]
        std_threshold = tree_.threshold[node_id]
        original_threshold = convert_threshold(feature_idx, std_threshold)
        
        # Create the rule strings for left and right branches
        left_rule = f"{feature_name} ≤ {original_threshold:.2f}"
        right_rule = f"{feature_name} > {original_threshold:.2f}"
        
        # Process left branch (True condition)
        extract_rules(
            tree_.children_left[node_id], 
            depth + 1,
            parent_rules + [left_rule]
        )
        
        # Process right branch (False condition)
        extract_rules(
            tree_.children_right[node_id], 
            depth + 1,
            parent_rules + [right_rule]
        )
    
    print("\n===== DECISION TREE RULES WITH ORIGINAL THRESHOLDS =====\n")
    extract_rules(0, 0)
    print("\n=======================================================\n")

# Usage:
print_decision_tree_rules(
    tree_model, 
    feature_names, 
    class_names=["No Disease", "Disease"], 
    scaler=scaler,  # you need to pass your scaler object here
    max_depth=3
)
```

If you also want a more concise, tabular format that shows the most important decision paths, you can add this alternative function:

```python
def print_decision_paths_summary(tree_model, feature_names, class_names, scaler):
    """
    Print a summary of the main decision paths in a tabular format
    with thresholds in the original scale
    """
    # Get the scaler parameters
    means = scaler.mean_
    stds = scaler.scale_
    
    # Get tree structure
    tree_ = tree_model.tree_
    
    # Get paths from root to leaves
    n_nodes = tree_.node_count
    children_left = tree_.children_left
    children_right = tree_.children_right
    feature = tree_.feature
    threshold = tree_.threshold
    
    # Function to convert standardized threshold to original scale
    def convert_threshold(feature_idx, std_threshold):
        return (std_threshold * stds[feature_idx]) + means[feature_idx]
    
    # Identify all paths from root to leaf
    def find_paths(node_id=0, path=[], paths=[]):
        path = path + [node_id]
        
        # If leaf node, save path
        if children_left[node_id] == children_right[node_id]:
            paths.append(path)
            return
        
        # Recursively process child nodes
        find_paths(children_left[node_id], path, paths)
        find_paths(children_right[node_id], path, paths)
        return paths
    
    # Get all paths
    paths = find_paths()
    
    # Print paths in a structured format
    print("\n===== DECISION PATHS SUMMARY =====\n")
    print(f"{'Path':<5} | {'Decision Rules':<50} | {'Prediction':<15} | {'Samples':<10} | {'Prob':<6}")
    print("-" * 90)
    
    for i, path in enumerate(paths):
        # Extract rules in this path
        rules = []
        for j in range(len(path) - 1):
            node_id = path[j]
            child_id = path[j + 1]
            
            feature_idx = feature[node_id]
            feature_name = feature_names[feature_idx]
            std_threshold = threshold[node_id]
            original_threshold = convert_threshold(feature_idx, std_threshold)
            
            if child_id == children_left[node_id]:
                rules.append(f"{feature_name} ≤ {original_threshold:.2f}")
            else:
                rules.append(f"{feature_name} > {original_threshold:.2f}")
        
        # Get leaf node prediction and samples
        leaf_id = path[-1]
        value = tree_.value[leaf_id][0]
        n_samples = tree_.n_node_samples[leaf_id]
        class_idx = np.argmax(value)
        class_prob = value[class_idx] / np.sum(value)
        
        # Format the rules for display
        rules_text = " AND ".join(rules)
        if len(rules_text) > 47:
            rules_text = rules_text[:44] + "..."
        
        print(f"{i+1:<5} | {rules_text:<50} | {class_names[class_idx]:<15} | {n_samples:<10} | {class_prob:.2f}")
    
    print("\n=================================\n")

# Usage:
print_decision_paths_summary(
    tree_model, 
    feature_names, 
    class_names=["No Disease", "Disease"],
    scaler=scaler  # pass your scaler object
)
```

Both functions will give you interpretable rules from your decision tree, with thresholds converted back to the original scale. The first function provides a more detailed, hierarchical representation, while the second gives a more concise tabular summary.

Note: Make sure to pass the same `scaler` object that you used to standardize your training data.
